narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream of becoming a lawyer, and we appreciate the effort you've put into your application, including your choice to commit to full-time studies. The admissions model we use predicts your likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 45% chance of failure for you, which crossed the threshold for rejecting your application to help ensure we select students most likely to succeed. This prediction was driven primarily by a few key aspects of your academic profile compared to others in our dataset. Your Year 3 school decile ranking stood out as the strongest positive factor, performing around average but pushing the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass than it otherwise would. However, your LSAT score, which is notably below the typical applicant's, had the biggest negative pull, strongly nudging the prediction toward failure. Your undergraduate GPA, also lower than average, added to that concern by further increasing the estimated risk. Meanwhile, your Year 1 decile, family income in the middle bracket, and full-time status all offered mild positives, leaning slightly toward success but not enough to offset the main concerns. Overall, compared to typical students who get approved—those with higher LSATs and GPAs around 3.21 and above—your scores placed you at a disadvantage in the model's eyes, even with your solid later-year performance. We know these metrics don't capture your full potential, resilience, or unique perspective as a Black woman ready to advocate in the legal field. To strengthen future applications, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, building on your improving deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted prep resources tailored to your background. You're capable of turning this around—many do—and we encourage you to reapply after addressing these areas. Keep pushing forward; your story matters.
